answer:
In Cynewulf's signed poems (*Andreas*, *Elene*, and *Juliana*), **dryhten** is a frequent epithet (appearing dozens of times across the three works) for God or Christ as supreme ruler, savior, and heavenly lord.[^1][^2][^3] It carries connotations of sovereign authority (*sigedryhten*, victory-lord), merciful protector (*ece dryhten*, eternal lord), and object of praise amid suffering (*wuldres dryhten*, glory-lord), aligning with the poems' hagiographic themes of martyrdom, divine intervention, and heroic devotion.[^4] For instance, in *Andreas*, it describes Christ guiding the apostle across perilous seas and sustaining him against cannibals;[^5] in *Elene*, the True Cross quest invokes it as the "heofona heahcyning" (high-king of heavens);[^6] and in *Juliana*, the saint binds a devil while praising the "ece dryhten" as soul's guardian.[^7]

The Old English Elegies anthology (17 poems/chapters, anonymous) uses **dryhten** less pervasively but similarly, blending secular lordship (e.g., lost human ring-giver) with Christian divine rule, emphasizing transience, exile, and God's ultimate mercy.[^8] Occurrences cluster in pious reflections: e.g., "dryhtenbealo" (lord-bale, divine suffering) in an untitled fragment;[^9] warnings against neglecting one's "dryhten" (lord/God) in "Maxims I" and "The Seafarer"-like pieces;[^10] and pleas for the "ece dryhten" (eternal lord) in "Resignation."[^11] It underscores wyrd (fate) under God's dominion, as in soul's fear of divine judgment.[^12] Not all elegies feature it prominently (*The Wanderer* lacks it, focusing on earthly *dryhten* loss via periphrasis).[^13]

Overall, **dryhten** bridges heroic and Christian idioms: a feudal lord elevated to cosmic sovereign (often Christ), invoked for solace amid *wraeclastas* (exile paths). The corpus covers this richly in Cynewulf but sparser in elegies; no exhaustive concordance exists here.

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch. 1 (transliteration), [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/#p-992aeb).
[^2]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, ch. 1 (transliteration), [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/#p-624562).
[^3]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, ch. 1 (transliteration), [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/#p-01394b).
[^4]: E.g., *Andreas*: "ece dryhten" as sea-calmer; *Juliana*: devil fears "dryhtna dryhten."
[^5]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/#p-992aeb).
[^6]: *Elene*, ch. 1, [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/#p-624562).
[^7]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/#p-01394b).
[^8]: *Old English Elegies*, chs. 1–9 (transliteration), multiple [paragraphs](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/#p-0e2690) et al.
[^9]: Ch. 2, [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/02/#p-0e2690).
[^10]: Chs. 5–6, [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05/#p-f25ee3); [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06/#p-0fe1ec).
[^11]: Ch. 9, [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/09/#p-ef37f8).
[^12]: Ch. 5, [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05/#p-f25ee3).
[^13]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [full chapter](https://falsafa.corpus/transliteration/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11/).
